mnemonics.title: TLS Handshake Steps
mnemonics.content: CHASE: ClientHello → ServerHello → Authentication → Session Key Exchange → Finished. Remember: 'Clever Hands Always Serve Excellent Feedback' - ClientHello, HelloVerifyRequest, Certificate, ServerKeyExchange, CertificateRequest, ServerHelloDone, Certificate, ClientKeyExchange, CertificateVerify, Finished.

mnemonics.title: Cipher Suite Components
mnemonics.content: Remember as 'KEMAA': Key Exchange Method + Encryption Algorithm + Message Authentication (MAC/Hash). Example: TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256

acronyms.usage: Cryptographic protocol for secure network communication
Successor to SSL, IETF standard protocol
Block cipher mode of operation in TLS
Key exchange using elliptic curve cryptography
Property ensuring past sessions remain secure

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: Client sends ClientHello → Server responds with ServerHello+Certificate → Server may request client cert → Client sends Certificate (if requested) → ClientKeyExchange → CertificateVerify → ChangeCipherSpec → Finished → Server sends ChangeCipherSpec → Finished. Show arrows between client and server with message names.

Draw a layered diagram: Top layer (Application Data) → Fragmentation → Compression → MAC (using HMAC) → Encryption → Add Record Header (Content Type, Version, Length). Show the transformation step by step.
Remember as: TLS + [Key Exchange] + _WITH_ + [Encryption] + [Hash]. Example: TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 breaks into: Key=ECDHE, Auth=RSA, Enc=AES-256-GCM, Hash=SHA384

analogies.analogy: It's like meeting someone for the first time and exchanging ID cards (certificates), agreeing on a secret language (cipher suite), and creating a private code (session keys) - all before discussing anything sensitive.

Think of a digital certificate like a government-issued passport. The passport proves your identity because a trusted authority (government) vouches for you. Similarly, CAs (like VeriSign) vouch for website identities.

It's like having a VIP pass at a conference. Instead of going through security and verification every time you enter a session room, you show your pass and get immediate access. Session tickets work similarly.

analogies.anal analogy: Imagine if someone stole your house keys, they could enter your home. But if you also had a unique safe code for each day, even if they get today's key, they can't enter yesterday's house. PFS ensures each session has unique keys.

analogies.analogy: Think of TLS Record Protocol as a secure envelope. First, your message is divided into manageable pieces (fragmentation), then placed in an envelope with a wax seal (MAC), and finally the envelope is locked (encryption) before sending.
